channel.title: NYT > U.S. > Politics
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: D.C. Court Strikes Down Local Ban on High-Capacity Gun Magazines
item.description: A three-judge panel reversed the conviction of a man charged with possessing a 30-bullet magazine, saying it had constitutional protection.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Rich Pedroncelli/Associated Press
item.media:description: A semi-automatic hand gun with a 10 shot magazine, left, and a 15 shot magazine, right, at a gun store in California.

item.title: Representative Tony Gonzales Will Not Seek Re-election
item.description: Several House members had called for Mr. Gonzales, a Texas Republican, to step down after texts emerged showing his pursuit of a staff member who later killed herself.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Samuel Corum/Getty Images
item.media:description: Representative Tony Gonzales at a House Homeland Security Committee hearing in Washington last month.

item.title: DOJ Releases Missing Interviews With Woman Who Made Claims Against Trump in Epstein Files
item.description: The pages had been withheld from the trove of documents related to the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein because of what officials called a mistaken determination that they were duplicates.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department acknowledged on Thursday that it had also identified about a dozen other documents that were “incorrectly coded as duplicative.”

item.title: Timeline: The Rise and Fall of Kristi Noem as Trump’s DHS Secretary
item.description: The homeland security secretary, who was fired by President Trump Thursday, helped fulfill his border pledges, but also drew negative attention to his administration.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Noem testified on Capitol Hill this week.

item.title: Minnesota Governor Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Welcome Kristi Noem’s Firing
item.description: Mayor Jacob Frey of Minneapolis said “Good riddance” after Ms. Noem’s ouster. Gov. Tim Walz and others said they still wanted a “complete overhaul” of the department.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra and Ernesto Londoño
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Tim Walz said Kristi Noem had done “a stunning amount of damage” during her tenure as homeland security secretary.

item.title: State Department Is Accused of Slow Assistance in Iran War for Americans Abroad
item.description: Many veteran diplomats faulted the State Department for its response after the attacks in Iran began, and for its actions beforehand.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kevin Lamarque/Reuters
item.media:description: Passengers evacuated from the Middle East arrived at Dulles International Airport on Thursday.

item.title: Steve Daines’s Switcheroo Starts New Fight in Montana Senate Race
item.description: Minutes before the Senate filing deadline, Senator Steve Daines withdrew his re-election bid and an ally jumped in. Even some fellow Republicans criticized the 11th-hour switch.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Thom Bridge/Independent Record, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Kurt Alme, who has been serving as the U.S. attorney for Montana, was endorsed by Senator Steve Daines and President Trump to succeed Mr. Daines in a race for what will now be an open seat.

item.title: Senator Blumenthal Pushes to Investigate Kristi Noem for Perjury
item.description: Senator Richard Blumenthal of Connecticut said Democrats had evidence to suggest Kristi Noem lied under oath during a Senate hearing on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Richard Blumenthal speaking on Capitol Hill in January. Blumenthal said that Kristi Noem’s removal did not protect her from an investigation.

item.title: S.E.C. Settles Its Case Against Justin Sun, an Investor in the Trumps’ Crypto Coins
item.description: The decision to settle the court action is particularly striking because the defendant was accused of a serious violation of securities laws. He denied the charges.
item.dc:creator: Sharon LaFraniere and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justin Sun, founder of the crypto platform TRON, on his way to the White House for a private tour last year. Mr. Sun threw Mr. Trump’s fledgling crypto company a cash lifeline at a time when it was struggling.

item.title: Trump Pick Of Mullin For D.H.S. Sets Off Political Scramble in Oklahoma
item.description: The president’s choice of Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma to replace her at the Department of Homeland Security could trigger a cascade of aspirants for his seat.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma’s term expires at the end of this year, so whoever is appointed to fill his seat will be a short termer in the Senate.

item.title: Who Is Markwayne Mullin, Trump’s Pick for Homeland Security Secretary?
item.description: The Oklahoma senator has been a staunch defender of the Trump administration’s mass deportation campaign.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin, Republican of Oklahoma, at the Capitol on Thursday.

item.title: Israel Begins Assault in Southern Beirut, Raising Fears of Wider Incursion
item.description: As the war with Iran threatens to engulf more countries in the region, President Trump said he wanted to be involved in picking a new leader in Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward, Aurelien Breeden, Erika Solomon and Thomas Fuller
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Diego Ibarra Sanchez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Smoke rising after an Israeli airstrike in the southern suburbs of Beirut, Lebanon, late Thursday and early Friday local time.

item.title: House Turns Back Bid to Restrict Trump’s War Powers in Iran
item.description: A handful of Democrats joined Republicans to defeat an effort to force President Trump to go to Congress for approval to continue using force against Iran, while two G.O.P. lawmakers backed it.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Thomas Massie of Kentucky was one of two Republicans to back the effort.

item.title: As Noem Speaks in Nashville, Senate Swarms Mullin
item.description: The remarkable split-screen underscored how abruptly Kristi Noem’s ouster as homeland security secretary had unfolded, even by the standards of President Trump’s fast-paced Washington.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson

item.title: FEMA Delays Under Kristi Noem Angered Republicans
item.description: Long delays in FEMA assistance brought bipartisan complaints, especially in North Carolina after Hurricane Helene in 2024.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Mike Belleme for The New York Times
item.media:description: Republican lawmakers from North Carolina had expressed frustration over how slowly FEMA was releasing disaster aid after Hurricane Helene devastated the state in 2024.

item.title: Pokémon Company Objects to White House’s Political Memes
item.description: “No permission was granted for the use of our intellectual property,” the company said. The Trump administration frequently promotes policies with content from video games.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The White House used an image from the new game Pokémon Pokopia in a social media post saying “Make America Great Again.”

item.title: Feeling the Effects of 260,000 Federal Jobs Lost
item.description: One year in, assessing budget cuts to federal climate and science jobs.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
item.media:description: Demonstrators gathered to protest against budget cuts outside the headquarters of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration in Silver Spring, Maryland, last year.

item.title: Lindsey Halligan Is Under Investigation by the Florida Bar
item.description: The actions of Ms. Halligan, who as a U.S. attorney brought criminal cases against President Trump’s enemies, are under review by the organization that licensed her to practice law.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Jacquelyn Martin/Associated Press
item.media:description: Lindsey Halligan at the White House last August.

item.title: How Trump’s Base Is Reacting to His War Message
item.description: Our White House correspondent Zolan Kanno-Youngs looks into how Trump’s base is responding to the administration’s conflicting messages on the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Gilad Thaler, Thomas Vollkommer, Laura Salaberry and Javier Manzano
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump Says He Should Have Role in Choosing Iran’s Next Leader
item.description: The president’s comments were the most detailed he has been about his vision of a U.S. role in creating a new government in Tehran.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said Mojtaba Khamenei, a son of the former Iranian leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei who appeared to be the leading candidate to succeed his father, was an “unacceptable” choice.

item.title: Senate Democrats Block D.H.S. Funding Again Over Enforcement Guardrails
item.description: For the third time, a bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security failed as Democrats refused to support the money without new restrictions on federal immigration agents.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Democrats’ list of demands includes requiring immigration officers to show visible identification, blocking them from wearing masks and adopting stricter use-of-force policies.

item.title: Trump Announces He Is Replacing Noem With Oklahoma Senator
item.description: The president said he would replace Kristi Noem, the homeland security secretary, with Senator Markwayne Mullin, after Ms. Noem was grilled by Republicans at a congressional hearing.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender, Michael Gold, Hamed Aleaziz and Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kristi Noem testifying before lawmakers this week on Capitol Hill.

item.title: House Republicans Tell Gonzales to Quit Re-Election Campaign
item.description: The call came a day after the Texas congressman admitted to an extramarital affair with an aide, and as he faced a runoff to keep his seat.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Tony Gonzales, Republican of Texas, publicly acknowledged on Wednesday a sexual relationship with a staff member who later took her own life.

item.title: States Sue to Stop Trump From Reviving Steep Tariffs
item.description: The lawsuit filed by two dozen attorneys general seeks to invalidate the president’s new, 10 percent global tax on imports.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The legal challenge is only the latest twist in a saga that has engulfed President Trump’s economic agenda, which hinges on the use of tariffs.

item.title: Board Delays Vote on Trump’s Ballroom Amid Deluge of Public Criticism
item.description: The National Capital Planning Commission, led by Trump allies, moved the vote to next month and listened to hours of public testimony, much of it negative, about the plan.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with a model of his proposed ballroom. He has moved to eliminate resistance to his plans, filling the boards and commissions meant to oversee the project with people who have worked for him.

item.title: An Assertive Supreme Court Turns to Curbing State Courts
item.description: Liberal justices accused their colleagues of expanding use of the emergency docket again in two orders issued this week.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Since President Trump returned to the White House, the Supreme Court’s conservative majority has shown a willingness to short-circuit lower-court proceedings.

item.title: How $800 Monthly Car Payments Are Hurting Car Sales
item.description: Cars have become so expensive that many Americans are putting off or not buying new cars, hurting the auto industry.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
item.media:credit: Jeff Roberson/Associated Press
item.media:description: The average monthly payment for a new car was $774 at the end of last year, and the average new car now costs nearly $50,000.

item.title: Republicans Toil to Avoid Saying ‘War’ as Iran Conflict Widens
item.description: Mission? Hostilities? Don’t call it a war, say G.O.P. lawmakers grappling with the political and legal challenges of the operation in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin, Republican of Oklahoma, arriving at the Capitol on Tuesday for a briefing on the Iran attacks. “This is war, and we’re taking out the threat,” he said before later backtracking.

item.title: Trump Wants a Quick Victory in Iran. But the War May Be Costly.
item.description: The opening days of the conflict are challenging the idea that President Trump can project force abroad while safeguarding American lives and the economy.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Heavy smoke rising from Tehran on Tuesday after U.S.-Israeli airstrikes.

item.title: Once a Bipartisan Stalwart, AIPAC Turns ‘Toxic’ in the Illinois Primaries
item.description: The intervention of AIPAC supporters in Chicago-area Democratic primaries, including one with opposing Jewish candidates, has made the pro-Israel lobby an issue on the left.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman and Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Joshua Lott for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left to right, Mayor Daniel Biss of Evanston, Ill., State Senator Laura Fine of Illinois and Kat Abughazaleh at Fox Chicago’s debate last month.

item.title: How the Decision to Start a War Became the President’s
item.description: Presidents have sidestepped Congress to launch limited military strikes for decades. Trump’s decision to attack Iran is an aggressive escalation.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: F.D.A. Faces Upset Over Denials of New Drugs
item.description: Agency officials promise fast reviews of new treatments while vowing they will not be a “rubber stamp” for the industry. But patients with rare diseases view recent decisions as signs that the doors are closing on their options.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett
item.media:credit: Will Crooks for The New York Times
